main_summary.summary: Dr. Cleveland L. Sellers Jr, interviewed by John Dittmer in Denmark, South Carolina, recounts his trajectory from Voorhees College mascot at age three to SNCC program secretary and Voorhees College president. Sellers describes growing up in a segregated Denmark shaped by Voorhees's Booker T. Washington and Du Bois influences, his shock at the Emmett Till murder, and his organizing of a Denmark NAACP youth chapter as a high school student. At Howard University he joined the Nonviolent Action Group, helped recruit Bayard Rustin and Malcolm X to the Lyceum program, and worked on March on Washington logistics under Rustin. He recounts the Cambridge, Maryland confrontations with George C. Wallace and the National Guard, recruiting volunteers for Mississippi Freedom Summer 1964, joining the Meridian-to-Philadelphia search for Goodman, Michael "Mickey" Schwerner, and Chaney, and directing the Holly Springs project. He details the Atlantic City MFDP challenge collapse, Walter Mondale's compromise, Fannie Lou Hamer's testimony, SNCC's response to Bloody Sunday after John Lewis's injury, the Lowndes County Freedom Organization that seeded the Black Panther symbol, and a private four-to-five-hour meeting in Atlanta where he, Stokely Carmichael, and Willie Ricks explained Black Power to Dr. King.
key_themes: SNCC field organizing and leadership transition
Mississippi Freedom Summer and the search for Goodman, Schwerner, and Chaney
Mississippi Freedom Democratic Party and the Atlantic City challenge
Lowndes County Freedom Organization and Black Panther symbol origins
Black Power articulation and the SNCC-SCLC relationship
main_summary.historical_significance:
Sellers offers first-person testimony on multiple pivot points where SNCC's strategic direction shifted: the Atlantic City compromise that ended SNCC's faith in coalition liberalism, the post-Selma decision to build independent political organizations in the Alabama Black Belt, and the Lowndes County Freedom Organization that exported the Black Panther symbol to Oakland and other cities. His account of the Meridian assembly after Goodman, Schwerner, and Chaney disappeared, the four-direction entry into Mississippi, and the two-night search of barns in Neshoba County documents internal SNCC decision-making that rarely appears in standard movement histories.

The interview also preserves Sellers's direct testimony about a private Atlanta meeting where he, Stokely Carmichael, and Willie Ricks spent four to five hours with Dr. King explaining Black Power after Cicero, Illinois, and the negotiated agreement that SCLC and SNCC would not publicly condemn each other. Sellers notes David Garrow could not corroborate the meeting with other King lieutenants, making this oral testimony the principal surviving record of that exchange.

chapters.title: Growing Up in Denmark
chapters.summary: Sellers describes Denmark, South Carolina, the cultural centrality of Voorhees College in his childhood, and the rigid segregation of the rural community. He explains how the Voorhees campus served as the area's high school under state funding, blending the Booker T. Washington industrial-education model with Du Bois's influence.
related_events: Founding of Voorhees College by a Tuskegee graduate

chapters.title: Emmett Till and the African Connection
chapters.summary: Sellers, eleven years old when Emmett Till was murdered, describes classroom discussions of the Jet magazine cover and the determination among Voorhees-area students to find remedies rather than revenge. He links this to the simultaneous independence movements of Kwame Nkrumah, Patrice Lumumba, and Jomo Kenyatta that African students at Voorhees brought into campus conversation.

chapters.title: Family and Church
chapters.summary: Sellers describes his father, a Denmark entrepreneur who built a motel and served in France during World War II, and his mother, a South Carolina State and Hampton graduate who taught at Voorhees for twenty years and ran the Denmark Recreation Center. He traces his family's religious journey across AME, Baptist, and Episcopal affiliations and the Episcopal Church's later support of his SNCC work.
related_events: World War II service in France

chapters.title: First Sit-Ins and NAACP Youth
chapters.summary: Sellers, then a high school student working in the Voorhees student center, describes how the February 1960 Greensboro sit-ins drew nightly news audiences and prompted Voorhees students to plan their own demonstration. He recounts the college president withdrawing the bus under board pressure, the seven-student march downtown, and his organizing a Denmark NAACP youth chapter against his father's objections, which prompted him to apply unsuccessfully to Staunton Military Academy.

chapters.title: Howard and NAG
chapters.summary: Sellers arrives at Howard in fall 1962 expecting a hotbed and instead finds a bourgeois campus. He joins the Nonviolent Action Group through Mike Thelwell, Stanley Wise, and Charlie Cobb, and describes NAG's picketing of the Justice Department, FBI, and White House over voter rights enforcement, plus local Washington D.C. home-rule and bus-desegregation campaigns. NAG members targeted leadership posts on the Hilltop newspaper, the Lyceum program, and student government.
related_events: NAG picketing of Justice Department and FBI

chapters.title: March on Washington
chapters.summary: Sellers recounts NAG securing Bayard Rustin and then Malcolm X as Howard Lyceum speakers, and Rustin recruiting NAG members to handle March on Washington logistics. He describes the controversy over John Lewis's speech draft, Cardinal Patrick O'Boyle's objection to the Sherman-burning-the-South metaphor, A. Philip Randolph's personal appeal to SNCC, and the post-march euphoria shattered by the 16th Street Baptist Church bombing.

chapters.title: Cambridge, Maryland
chapters.summary: Sellers describes the Cambridge, Maryland movement under Gloria Richardson, the imposition of martial law, and the night George C. Wallace was invited to speak. He, Stokely Carmichael, Stanley Wise, and Gloria were arrested and held overnight in the National Guard armory. He recounts the National Guard's tear-gas-and-nausea-agent mixture and the bayonet wounding of SNCC photographer Cliff Vaughs as the demonstration tried to march to Wallace's venue.

chapters.title: Freedom Summer and the Search
chapters.summary: Sellers recounts Bob Moses assigning NAG to recruit Freedom Summer volunteers from Maryland and D.C. campuses, the Oxford, Ohio orientation, and Moses's announcement that Goodman, Schwerner, and Chaney were missing and presumed dead. He describes Jean Wheeler's song breaking the silence, then a small group including Stokely Carmichael, Charlie Cobb, Ralph Featherstone, Dona Richards, and Gwen Robinson entering Mississippi from four directions to search for bodies at night from a Neshoba County barn loft over two nights before pulling back.
related_events: Disappearance of Goodman Schwerner and Chaney
Mt. Zion church burning Philadelphia Mississippi

chapters.title: Holly Springs Project
chapters.summary: Sellers describes the Holly Springs project covering five or six northern Mississippi counties, with three Boston master teachers, a nurse, a freedom school, a community center, and the largest MFDP application count of any project. Ivanhoe Donaldson directed it through summer; Sellers took over in fall 1964 and mentored youth including Roy DeBerry and SNCC volunteers including Larry Rubin and Peter Cummings of the Harvard Crimson. Records were later archived at Rust College.

chapters.title: Atlantic City and the MFDP Compromise
chapters.summary: Sellers describes hauling the burned Mt. Zion church bell and the burned Goodman-Schwerner-Chaney station wagon to Atlantic City as visual evidence, the Johnson administration delegating containment to Humphrey and Walter Mondale, and the two-seats-at-large compromise. He recounts Johnson cutting away national TV coverage during Fannie Lou Hamer's testimony with an impromptu press conference, the delegation's rejection of the compromise, and SNCC's loss of faith in the morality of Democratic Party coalition politics.

chapters.title: Selma and Bloody Sunday
chapters.summary: Sellers describes the SNCC staff debate over whether to participate in the SCLC-led Selma campaign, John Lewis's insistence on marching personally, and the rapid SNCC mobilization from a Mississippi MFDP meeting after Bloody Sunday left Lewis with a concussion. He took over logistics with Hosea Williams and brought in Willie "Mukasa" Ricks; SNCC was frustrated that King's turnaround march was not communicated to them. Jim Forman shifted demonstrations to Montgomery.

chapters.title: Lowndes County and the Black Panther
chapters.summary: Sellers describes Stokely Carmichael's organizing in Lowndes County, Alabama after Selma, and Jack Minnis's research department discovery of a Civil War era Alabama statute permitting independent county parties. SNCC organized the Lowndes County Freedom Organization, which the press dubbed the Black Panther Party. Young men brought down from Philadelphia, Chicago, New York, and California as poll watchers carried the symbol home, seeding Bobby Seale and Huey Newton's Oakland party.
related_events: Black Panther Party of Oakland formation

chapters.title: Black Power and Dr. King
chapters.summary: Sellers reflects on SNCC's transformation after Samuel Younge Jr's January 1966 killing and SNCC's anti-war statement, the membership shift from majority-black to majority-white, COINTELPRO disruption, and his own three-month imprisonment for draft refusal. He recounts a private four-to-five-hour Atlanta basement meeting after Cicero, Illinois where he, Stokely Carmichael, and Willie Ricks explained Black Power to Dr. King, reaching a mutual-non-condemnation agreement that David Garrow could not corroborate with other King lieutenants.

chapters.title: Reflection and Career
chapters.summary: Sellers, asked to summarize his last forty years, describes movement work as a university-equivalent education through contact with Archibald Cox, Dr. King, Whitney Young, Malcolm X, Ella Baker, Fannie Lou Hamer, and Ruby Doris Smith Robinson. He earned a master's at Harvard and a doctorate at UNC, returning to Voorhees as president in 2008. The interviewer references Sellers's autobiography The River of No Return as the principal complementary source.
related_events: Publication of The River of No Return
